structure.notes: The required Stage 0 roles and PartialID locked-parameter block are mostly present, and the section-7 bounding-functional usage is cluster-appropriate. However, the note omits the in-repository prior-work positioning present in the Stage -1 proposal, and the formalization checklist does not explicitly separate the law-invariant probabilistic assumptions from the deterministic LP/inequality objects needed to police the density-version issue.

novelty.notes: The actual derivation is at most subfield/incremental relative to the novelty_target=field floor. Once pointwise sharp intervals and absence of cross-dose compatibility restrictions are assumed, the main equality is essentially product/coupling plus intersection with a finite monotone-concave cone; the shape tightening is a standard LP projection phenomenon. The note does not establish a genuinely new continuous-dose sensitivity regime beyond the locked grid-version construction.

correctness.notes: The main pasting proof relies on assigning continuous-treatment density values at finitely many points and using those version-dependent values as substantive restrictions. This must be made mathematically explicit as a version-level model primitive, or the theorem is not a law-invariant partial-identification statement. Proposition 2's proof also asserts feasibility of a displayed vector that need not be monotone when L1>L2, although the formula may be repairable by a different feasible completion.

perItemFindings.label: Theorem \ref{thm:sharp-grid} / Lemma \ref{lem:pasting}
perItemFindings.one_line: The reverse-inclusion proof changes or prescribes density values at finitely many continuous-dose points; without treating density versions as part of the model, the restriction is not a property of the full-data law.

perItemFindings.label: Novelty tier
perItemFindings.one_line: Assessed tier is subfield or incremental, below novelty_target=field; the kernel is generic finite-grid shape closure of assumed sharp pointwise intervals, not a field-level new bound or regime.

perItemFindings.label: Related work and positioning
perItemFindings.one_line: The note omits the Stage -1 in-repository comparison and does not sufficiently distinguish the theorem from standard Manski-style completion plus shape-restricted LP projection.

perItemFindings.label: Proposition \ref{prop:three-dose}
perItemFindings.one_line: The proof claims the vector (L0,max{L1,c},L2) is feasible under the stated condition, but this vector can violate monotonicity when L1>L2.

perItemFindings.label: Assumption \ref{ass:pointwise}
perItemFindings.one_line: A major part of the sharpness burden is shifted into a labelled pointwise-sharpness assumption, leaving the proved contribution mainly as a coupling/pasting lemma under independent grid-dose restrictions.

review.verbatim_critique:
The note is structurally close to the required Stage 0 format for a PartialID anchor: it has the locked tuple (pid_dose_response_shape, v1, P, theta, R_{Gamma,mc}, B_{Gamma,mc}), labelled assumptions, a cluster-appropriate bounding functional in the fixed 'Regression estimator' section, a target-estimand section that does not define the target tautologically, main results, proofs, sanity checks, a minimality check, and a math-object checklist. The structural defects are secondary: the related-work section drops the in-repository comparison that was present in the proposal, and the formalization checklist should more explicitly expose the density-version/model-primitive issue as a probabilistic object rather than treating it as an ordinary deterministic pasting identity.

The main correctness concern is Lemma \ref{lem:pasting}. The construction sets f_{Q_v}(a_j | X,U)=e(a_j | X)q_j(U,X) at finitely many design doses and then says that changing density values on a finite set does not change the integrated conditional law of A. That observation is true, but it is also the problem: for a continuous treatment, density values at individual points are version-dependent and are not law-invariant features of Q. Thus the displayed complete-over-nominal ratio at A=a_j is being controlled by choosing a density version on a null set, not by a substantive restriction on the conditional treatment law, unless the locked model explicitly makes density versions part of the primitive admissible structure. The theorem can be correct only in that version-level/grid-specialized sense. As a journal-style partial-identification statement about laws, this is a serious gap because the asserted sharpness may be an artifact of modifiable density representatives.

The proof of Proposition \ref{prop:three-dose} has a local algebraic/feasibility gap. It lower-bounds mu_1 by max{L1,c(L0,L2)}, but then asserts that the vector (L0,max{L1,c},L2) is feasible under the proposition's upper-endpoint condition. If L1>L2, that displayed vector violates monotonicity even when the full polytope is nonempty. The proposition may be repairable by choosing the third coordinate as max{L2,m} with m=max{L1,c}, subject to the existing nonemptiness/upper feasibility implications, but the current proof is not correct as written.

The novelty claim does not clear the orchestrator's field-tier floor. The derivation assumes pointwise sharp no-shape intervals in Assumption \ref{ass:pointwise}; assumes no cross-dose smoothness or compatibility restrictions in the locked grid-specialized CMSM; then proves that independently realizable coordinates can be coupled and intersected with monotone-concave inequalities. That is useful as a finite-grid technical lemma, but it is not a field-level new sharp bound in the sense of resolving a previously open substantive continuous-dose sensitivity regime. The three-dose phase transition is a standard concavity chord inequality applied to interval endpoints. The note also does not adequately compare this kernel to ordinary Manski completion arguments, shape-restricted LP projection, or the in-repository PartialID artifacts cited in the Stage -1 proposal. Because the assessed tier_at_derivation is below field and the gap appears structural to the locked grid-version formulation, the artifact should not be accepted under novelty_target=field.

report_summary: Related work and positioning, lines 96-99:novelty, Theorem \ref{thm:sharp-grid}:novelty, Assumption \ref{ass:pointwise}:novelty, Lemma \ref{lem:pasting} and Stage -1 Conjecture 1:novelty, Proposition \ref{prop:three-dose}:novelty

structure.notes: The note has the required Stage 0 roles, preserves the PartialID locked tuple (pid_dose_response_shape, v1, P, theta, R_{Gamma,mc}, B_{Gamma,mc}) in the Formal setup block, uses labelled assumptions, treats the fixed 'Regression estimator' section as a bounding-functional section, and includes an actionable PartialID math-object checklist separating probabilistic objects from deterministic LP/inequality identities.

novelty.notes: The derivation explicitly downgrades the contribution to 'subfield or incremental, not field-level' at lines 96-99. The proved kernel is a generic finite-grid outer containment obtained by intersecting assumed pointwise sensitivity intervals with monotone-concave inequalities and projecting a polytope. Under novelty_target=field, this cannot be accepted; the failed law-level sharpness/pasting conjecture was the only plausible field-tier kernel, and it is refuted rather than proved.

correctness.notes: The main containment theorem is mathematically correct as an outer-bound statement under the labelled pointwise-envelope and shape assumptions. The three-dose proposition's chord algebra and repaired witness (L0,m,max{L2,m}) are correct under its stated feasibility conditions, and the Gamma=1 collapse is correct as a polytope statement.

perItemFindings.one_line: The flagship theorem proves only Theta_I(P) subset Theta_{Gamma,mc}(P), a routine outer containment once pointwise intervals and monotone-concavity are assumed.

perItemFindings.label: Assumption \ref{ass:pointwise}
perItemFindings.one_line: The pointwise sharp sensitivity intervals are imported as a primitive rather than derived, so the theorem-level contribution is the finite-dimensional LP closure, not a new sensitivity-identification result.

perItemFindings.label: Lemma \ref{lem:pasting} and Stage -1 Conjecture 1

review.verbatim_critique:
The artifact is structurally usable for later ThmSmith stages. It preserves the PartialID anchor convention, keeps the target causal estimand distinct from the bounding functional, labels the assumptions, gives proofs and sanity checks, and the Formalization checklist identifies the relevant probability objects, density-version issue, finite polytope, coordinate projections, and deterministic chord/LP facts.

The decisive failure is novelty under the orchestrator-enforced field floor. The actual theorem proved in Theorem \ref{thm:sharp-grid} is an outer inclusion: every law-level feasible vector must satisfy the pointwise no-shape intervals and the monotone-concave inequalities, hence lies in their intersection. This is correct, but it is exactly the generic Manski-style interval-completion plus shape-restricted LP projection template. The note imports the pointwise sharp sensitivity envelopes through Assumption \ref{ass:pointwise}; it does not derive a new continuous-treatment sensitivity bound. It also does not prove law-level sharpness of the shape-closed grid polytope.

The note is unusually candid about this limitation. In Related work and positioning, lines 96-99 state that the result is not a field-level new continuous-dose sharp bound and that the novelty tier is subfield or incremental. That assessment is right. The Stage -1 field-tier candidate was the normalization-pasting / joint sharpness claim, but Lemma \ref{lem:pasting} explains why the law-level reverse inclusion fails: changing density values at finitely many continuous-dose points changes only representatives, not the conditional treatment law. Repairing this to field tier would require a different primitive, such as a version-level model or a law-invariant neighborhood/limit restriction, which would change the locked problem rather than locally patch this note.

Correctness otherwise passes for the downgraded theorem. The outer containment proof is a direct consequence of pointwise interval containment and Assumption \ref{ass:mc}. Proposition \ref{prop:three-dose} correctly converts three-dose concavity into the endpoint chord lower bound and uses the repaired witness (L0,m,z) with z=max{L2,m}; the numerical sanity check at equally spaced doses gives the advertised lower bound 0.50. Proposition \ref{prop:gamma-one} is also correct as a finite-polytope collapse statement.

Because the assessed tier_at_derivation is below field and the gap is structural to the current law-level outer-bound formulation, the note must be rejected under novelty_target=field despite being structurally complete and mathematically sound as a lower-tier Stage 0 derivation.

structure.notes: The note matches the required Stage 0 skeleton: all required roles are present, the anchor is correctly treated as PartialID, the locked tuple is preserved in substance, assumptions are labelled, §7 is used as a bounding-functional section, and the Formalization checklist is actionable with probabilistic objects separated from deterministic LP/chord facts.

novelty.notes: The actual derivation falls below the orchestrator-enforced field floor. The only plausible field-tier kernel in the Stage -1 proposal was law-level joint sharpness of the shape-closed continuous-dose grid, but the Stage 0 theorem refutes that kernel and retains only a generic outer containment plus a density-version obstruction. The containment is standard interval-box ∩ shape-cone projection; the obstruction is useful but does not deliver a new sharp or strictly tighter PartialID bound.

correctness.notes: As a downgraded outer-bound note, the mathematics is correct: the inclusion follows from the pointwise interval primitive and monotone-concavity, the density-version lemma correctly identifies the finite-null-set representative problem, the three-dose chord formula and repaired witness are valid under the stated feasibility conditions, and the Γ=1 singleton collapse is correct as a polytope statement.

review.verbatim_critique:
The artifact is structurally reviewable and mostly well disciplined. It preserves the PartialID anchor, distinguishes the causal target θ from the bounding functional B_{Γ,mc}, labels the assumptions, and gives a usable §14 checklist. The §7 skeleton title is handled correctly for a PartialID note: it defines the bounding functional rather than a regression estimator.

The decisive problem is novelty under novelty_target=field. The Stage -1 field-level promise was a joint finite-grid sharpness theorem: every shape-compatible vector in the pointwise sensitivity box could be realized by one law-level continuous-treatment hidden-confounding model. Stage 0 instead proves that finite-point density-ratio pasting is not law-invariant and therefore cannot establish the reverse inclusion Θ_{Γ,mc}(P)⊆Θ_I(P). That is a valid negative diagnosis, but it collapses the flagship contribution rather than proving a new sharp bound.

What remains is Theorem \ref{thm:sharp-grid}(a): any feasible law-level target vector lies inside the product of imported pointwise intervals intersected with monotone-concave inequalities. This is the standard PartialID outer-envelope construction once Assumption \ref{ass:pointwise} supplies L_j and U_j. It is useful, but it is generic Manski-style interval completion plus a finite LP projection, not a new field-level continuous-dose identification result. Proposition \ref{prop:three-dose} is likewise correct but elementary: three-dose concavity is equivalent to the endpoint chord lower bound, and the repaired witness (L0,m,max{L2,m}) gives attainment under the stated upper-bound conditions.

Correctness otherwise passes for the downgraded claims. Lemma \ref{lem:pasting} correctly uses the fact that finite changes to a density representative at continuous dose points do not change the induced conditional law. The proof of the outer inclusion is a direct consequence of the pointwise-envelope and shape assumptions. The numerical sanity check with equally spaced doses correctly gives μ1≥0.50, and the Γ=1 proposition correctly yields a singleton polytope if the identified vector satisfies shape and the empty set otherwise.

Because the assessed tier_at_derivation is below field, the floor directive forbids ACCEPT. This is not a local exposition problem: to lift the note to field tier it would need either a new law-invariant sharpness theorem under the locked restrictions or a changed primitive such as neighborhood/limit restrictions or representative-level admissibility. Those would materially alter the current locked law-level problem. Therefore the appropriate verdict is reject for novelty, despite structural completeness and mathematical correctness as a lower-tier outer-bound note.

Producer correctly diagnosed and even reframed around the obstruction; the cap-exhausting flag is structural to the locked law-level formulation, not an upstream defect Stage 0/1/4d can fix.
